text: Highlight Clip SpaceX's Bitcoin Bet Is Larger Than Most People Thought On May 27, 2026, Bloomberg reported that SpaceX's latest regulatory filing (S-1) reveals the company currently holds 18,712 Bitcoin, with a total value reaching $1.3 billion. SpaceX treats these Bitcoin holdings as a treasury reserve asset, similar to cash or bonds. Ahead of what is anticipated to be the world's largest IPO, SpaceX's sophisticated finance team likely views this as a long-term hedge against inflation or a strategic reserve for excess cash. Furthermore, the filing indicates that SpaceX has not sold any Bitcoin since the end of 2024. — link

text: RFV Raid: How Patagon's Lawsuit, Overnight's Treasury Move & Circle's Blacklist Wrecked $12.6M of Zama Users Overnight Full story from today's drama 1. What does RFV (risk free value) mean? • Basically the assets that a protocol/DAO's treasury have (stablecoins) that could theoretically back every circulating token at a guaranteed value • "RFV raiding" / "RFV play" = find a struggling DAO/protocol where: treasury has lots of real money but governance token trades at a big discount (market cap << treasury value). Buy up cheap tokens → accumulate voting power (or just sue as a token holder) → force the DAO to wind down / distribute the treasury back to token holders (i.e., cash out the "risk-free" profits) • It's often low-risk/high-reward for the raiders because they buy discounted tokens, and legal/governance costs can sometimes be covered by the DAO itself ——— 2. What is Patagon LLC? • Patagon Management is a Delaware-based proprietary trading / activist investing firm known for RFV-style plays • They buy large stakes in cheap governance tokens of troubled projects (RFV >> MC) to push governance votes to liquidate/distribute treasury to holders • If the team/founder resists, file civil lawsuit + emergency TRO (Temporary Restraining Order) in US court • Court orders freezes/blacklists → assets secured → settlement/judgment → plaintiffs (including Patagon) recover their share + legal costs ——— 3. What actually happened with Zama • Starts with Overnight Finance where OVN holders vote to liquidate/distribute the treasury back to themselves • May 11: Founder Maxim Ermilov moves ~$15.77M out of treasury wallets → new address → bridges to Ethereum → deposits into Zama's cUSDC confidential/privacy wrapper contract • Three funds holding OVN tokens (incl. Patagon Management as co-plaintiff) file class-action suit accusing Ermilov of misappropriating treasury / trying to hide funds • They file emergency motion → Judge issues TRO ordering Circle to blacklist/freeze related USDC addresses/contracts • Plaintiffs allegedly link the disputed wallet to the Zama contract in court filings (Zach later questions whether they clearly disclosed it was a third-party shared privacy pool, not a personal bad-actor wallet) • Circle blacklists the entire public Zama cUSDC smart contract (address 0xe978F…72B2 - openly labeled as Zama's in docs/explorers) • Result: $12.6M USDC frozen in the pool (the ~$12.4M Overnight deposit + whatever other user funds were there • No prior notice to Zama team (huge issue - they only learned via ZachXBT) ——— 4. What ZachXBT found out & exposed • First alert: Circle ban list + Etherscan proof - Zama's public contract frozen, $12.6M user funds locked. • Follow-up: Overnight community post + on-chain proof - exact wallet (0xf7Fcc…) tied to Overnight deposited the $12.4M into Zama on May 11, right after the governance drama • Update 2: Zama had zero notice from Circle; plaintiffs may have misrepresented the address to the judge to get the TRO faster; Patagon is the plaintiff with known "hostile DAO takeover / RFV raiding" history. Zach says he feels bad for innocent Zama users caught in "this mess of a US civil case" ——— 5. Current status • Zama working with all parties to carve out/unfreeze. Court hearing Monday (June 1). Broader debate exploding about civil suits + Circle's compliance nuking DeFi/privacy pools • One disputed treasury move in a messy DAO → aggressive RFV-style lawsuit + US court TRO + Circle's all-or-nothing blacklist of a public commingled contract = $12.6M of random users' funds frozen overnight, with zero heads-up to the innocent protocol • Zach sees it as classic "arbitraging outdated laws + boomer judges" creating precedent that hurts the whole ecosystem for the profit/play of a few activists • Patagon see it as necessary protection for token holders against a founder absconding with funds ——— Links • • •

text: Circle suddenly froze/blacklisted a specific contract used by Zama's "Confidential USDC" (cUSDC) This froze ≈ $12.6 million of user funds inside Zama Circle did this as it's tied to a US civil lawsuit against Overnight Finance: a wallet linked to Overnight Finance had deposited roughly $12.4 million USDC into Zama's contract earlier in May The plaintiffs in that lawsuit (one of them is a group called Patagon Management, known in crypto for aggressive "DAO takeovers" and raiding protocols) got a court order (a Temporary Restraining Order) that caused Circle to freeze the Zama contract Funds from the two projects had apparently gotten mixed together (commingled). Zama's team was never notified beforehand by Circle or the plaintiffs. The plaintiffs may have misrepresented the situation to the court (they made it sound like the frozen address was directly part of Zama's protocol in a way that justified the freeze) This is a really bad precedent: it means a legal fight involving one project can suddenly lock up innocent users' money in a completely different protocol

text: ZachXBT: $12.6M in Zama cUSDC Frozen After Circle Blacklisting ZachXBT revealed that Circle may have blacklisted the contract address of privacy protocol Zama's confidential USDC (cUSDC) on Ethereum around seven hours ago, freezing approximately $12.6 million in user funds. Further analysis showed the address had recently participated in an Overnight Finance governance vote on treasury allocation. Some users had previously accused the project team of conducting a rug pull. ZachXBT also noted that one of the plaintiffs in a civil lawsuit against Overnight Finance is Patagon Management, an entity known for hostile DAO takeovers and attacks on protocol residual value. He suggested the plaintiff may have misrepresented the connection between the frozen address and the Zama contract to the court, while the Zama team appeared to have received no prior notice before the freeze was executed. — link Zama founder Rand stated that the root cause of the incident has been identified and is unrelated to the Zama protocol or privacy technology itself. It stemmed from $12.5 million in USDC previously deposited by an address linked to the Overnight Finance hack. At the time of deposit, the address was neither sanctioned nor flagged as high-risk by KYT tools, allowing the funds to enter the protocol. Because more than 99% of the cUSDC contract's funds originated from the address, the court ordered the entire wrapped contract to be frozen to prevent further movement of the disputed assets. Rand added that Zama has suspended the cUSDC, cUSDT, and cWETH wrapped asset contracts pending completion of the investigation and the adoption of appropriate measures.

text: ZeroTier CEO: Attackers Are Already 'Harvesting Now, Decrypting Later' Crypto Network Traffic for Quantum Era Andrew Gault, CEO of network firm ZeroTier, warned the Bitcoin industry's focus on quantum threats may be misguided. He stated the greatest risk isn't compromised wallet keys, but encrypted authentication data currently transmitted between institutions and exchanges. Gault noted attackers could adopt a "store now, decrypt later" strategy to hoard network traffic and digital signatures, making inter-institutional data transmission a severe challenge for the future financial system. — link

text: General Framework for Looking at Software Opportunities from First Principles Large enterprises adopt AI slowly due to compliance and budgeting constraints, meaning deeply embedded, vertical-specific software vendors that own proprietary customer workflows and data are highly insulated from DIY AI disruption and may gain new pricing power General-purpose, horizontal software providers (like Salesforce) are highly vulnerable because their core functionalities are easily replicated by AI, and institutional investors are increasingly penalizing their valuations by auditing stock-based compensation and actual GAAP margins

text: The Burden of Discernment The rapid commercialization of academic publishing and the deluge of digital data have severely strained the traditional peer-review system, leading to an increase in flawed research and scientific misconduct As generative AI further accelerates text production and automates knowledge generation, humanity must rebuild its collective capacity for critical judgment to ensure that we do not outsource our fundamental responsibility for discernment and truth

text: AI Disruption: Moats and Value Traps Investors are circling as stocks at risk of AI disruption fall to discounted valuations. However, traditional value metrics struggle when applied to firms facing disruption. Long-term winners of disruption often possess key intangible assets, both technological and complementary, not captured by traditional metrics Intangible value, which incorporates these moats, has been effective at separating disruption's winners from value traps. Applied to the software selloff, this framework reveals not a blanket bargain but a dispersion-rich stockpicking opportunity

text: Highlight Clip Michael Saylor: Keep Your Kidney, but Hold On to Your Bitcoin Strategy founder Michael Saylor said in a May 21 interview with When Shift Happens that Bitcoin is a stronger form of capital preservation than traditional assets. When liquidity is needed, he said people should first sell assets with shorter "half-lives," rather than Bitcoin that could benefit future generations. Saylor compared selling Bitcoin to buy a Ferrari to an ancestor giving up generational wealth for a fancy carriage. Responding to a viral meme, he added: "You should keep your kidney, but more importantly, hold on to your Bitcoin." — link
